TextENGNew: July 5th, Showa 58 (Tuesday)
changeReason: Date format changed from Western calendar year to Japanese Showa era dating, which alters how the reader contextualizes the time period.

TextENGNew: "...I'm sorry.@k@vS08/13/321000573. I didn't mean to take up so much of your time."
"Oh, not at all. Customers who buy in bulk are always welcome."
The woman at the station kiosk smiled cheerfully as she bagged up all the newspapers and magazines.
changeReason: Changed 'old woman' to 'woman'; おばさん refers to a middle-aged woman, not necessarily elderly.

TextENGNew: ...I returned an awkward, forced smile and pulled a ten-thousand yen note from my wallet, placing it on the coin tray.
"Still, this rain lately has been such a nuisance, coming and going like that.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1003. I can't even find time to bring in the laundry."
"Indeed.@k@vS08/13/321000575. It is the rainy season, after all."
"They say a typhoon's heading this way too, right?@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1005. Oh, I do hope it doesn't make landfall."
changeReason: Old translation says 'I might have to head to high ground'; new says 'I hope it doesn't make landfall.' The Japanese 上陸しなければいいんだけど means 'I hope it doesn't make landfall,' so the new translation corrects a mistranslation.

TextENGNew: "...Yes, quite."
I nodded vaguely and glanced at my wristwatch.
...I had intended it as a silent signal to say 'please hurry up,' but when I stole a glance at her, the woman's hands hadn't picked up speed at all.
changeReason: Changed 'old woman' to 'woman'; おばさん refers to a middle-aged woman, not necessarily elderly.

TextENGNew: (...Good grief.)

What a predicament. I'd figured it would be too much trouble to hunt down a bookstore in an unfamiliar town, so I'd stopped by the station kiosk for convenience... but it might have been quicker to find a proper bookstore after all.
My gaze happened to drift downward, where I noticed tabloid headline advertisements hanging from a rack.
Beside a 'Summer High School Baseball Special,' there it was -- taking up more than half the space, in enormous lettering: 'Chief Cabinet Secretary Okuno Resigns.'

It was good that the news had finally been made public, but the reason for the resignation remained a mystery. And our investigation, which was supposed to uncover the clues behind it... was proving extraordinarily difficult.
"By the way, sir -- could you be with a publishing company too?"
"Huh? ...What makes you say that?"
"Well, there was someone here last Friday too.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1008. Bought up a bunch of different magazines and newspapers all at once, just like you."
"Is... is that so?"
I caught my breath involuntarily and turned to face the shopkeeper with an urgency that nearly had me leaning over the counter.
Buying up newspapers and magazines in bulk... from this very kiosk, just like me?
"...What kind of person were they?"

"Can't really remember the face, but it was a woman in a suit.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1010. Even when I tried making conversation, she just kept scribbling in some notebook -- barely reacted at all.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1011. Gave off a rather cold impression."
"What made you think she was in the publishing business?"
"I caught a glimpse of a publisher's name on an envelope she was holding.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1013. Which publisher was it again...?"
"When I asked her, 'Checking up on the competition?' she just said, 'Something like that,' and clammed right up."
"A publisher..."
"So I figured maybe you're the same type.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1016. Am I wrong?"
Quite the nosy woman. ...Suppressing the agitation stirring inside me, I adjusted my hat and answered.
"Actually, I'm a haiku enthusiast.@k@vS08/13/321000586. I've been submitting to anything and everything, so I wanted to check if any of my work made it into print."
"Oh my... haiku?@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1018. All right, here you are -- that's everything."
"Thank you.@k@vS08/13/321000588. ...Ah, is this enough to cover it?"
As I finally received the two bags of newspapers and magazines she held out, I asked the question with the intention of wrapping up the conversation quickly.
The woman haltingly punched prices into an old calculator, tallied the total, and handed me my change.
"Thank you for your business.@k@vS08/00/VT2B_baitF1020. I hope your work made it in."
"Yes.@k@vS08/13/321000590. If not this time, I'll keep at it.@k@vS08/13/321000591. Well then, good day."
I gave her a smile and a slight bow, then turned on my heel.
The moment I turned my back, a cold shudder raced through my entire body -- one so intense even I could feel it.
(Just a coincidence? Or...)
I stopped in my tracks, thinking about going back and asking the shopkeeper more about that woman.
...But I immediately gave up on the idea.
Even if I got a description of her appearance, I had no way of finding her in a city this large.
And if the 'publishing' connection turned out to be a fabrication as well...
"But... the same as me, huh."
I might be overthinking things, but I filed her existence away in the back of my mind for now.
...That was about all I could do at the moment.
While waiting for the train on the platform, I pulled a few magazines out of the bag and skimmed through them.
Every magazine cover bore enormous headlines evoking the recent Great Hinamizawa Disaster, splashed in large print designed to seize the reader's attention.
"'The Cursed Village -- Tragedy of Hinamizawa!!'@k@vS08/13/321000596. 'Unprecedented Natural Disaster -- A Demon's Curse?!' ...Give me a break."
Despite their grandiose headlines, the actual articles were shoddy work.
Dubious precedents too suspicious to call proper analogies, and forced commentary from so-called experts I'd never heard of. Anyone with a shred of real knowledge would find this drivel laughable.

Moreover, as part of their supposed local coverage, the articles cited information obtained from 'a certain Hinamizawa insider' -- yet the source's identity was completely obscured, making me seriously doubt whether any real investigation had taken place.
After all, no one knew better than us just how difficult it was to get straight answers from these 'insiders.'
I didn't mean to claim our own efforts were beyond reproach, but I found it hard to believe these reporters had access to better sources than we did.
(At this point, it's less 'reporting' and more 'fiction.' ...This is no joke.)
...My frustration wasn't born solely from sympathy for Hinamizawa. Even from the standpoint of professional ethics, the fundamental approach to reporting here felt wrong.

The rigorous collection of reliable information, and the construction of sound logic derived from it -- then communicating all of that to the public in detail and with accuracy. That was supposed to be the duty incumbent upon journalism.
And yet, everything they had written about Hinamizawa was nothing but shallow, ad hoc profiteering -- content tailored exclusively to titillate the reader's curiosity.
Perhaps it was born from a certain sense of security -- the comfort of believing it had nothing to do with them.
But... I couldn't help feeling indignant at their utter lack of responsibility and awareness.
(...How vulgar.)
And that wasn't all.
Convenience store magazine racks, station kiosks, bookstores -- everywhere I went, headlines trumpeting keywords about Hinamizawa and the disaster were plastered about to an infuriating degree.
What's more, their overblown designs and exaggerated wording couldn't help but remind me of the bizarre decorations I'd seen at the Hatakeyama and Kimiyoshi houses...
"Even so... '2,000 sacrificial victims,' they say.@k@vS08/13/321000600. How casually they write such things..."
To them, the dead were merely a 'number' -- people with no personal connection, reduced to a figure that made their articles more exciting.
Without the slightest consideration that each of those 2,000 people had lived a life, with feelings and dreams of their own...
Before long, a local train pulled into the platform and came to a stop, its opening doors releasing a sparse trickle of passengers.
I boarded after the stream of people had passed, then leaned my back against the closed door on the opposite side.
Numerous hanging advertisements swayed gently in the moderately crowded car.
When I spotted the name of a certain magazine among them, I closed my eyes in disgust and resolved to look at nothing until I reached my destination...
I returned to the Kakiuchi police station and entered Conference Room 3 -- the space Inspector Minai had secured as our base of operations.
Inside, Ooishi-san was sipping tea while peering into a magazine spread open before him.
Beside his hand sat an ashtray overflowing with a mountain of crushed cigarette butts. He ground out yet another one atop the pile.
His hair was disheveled and his eyes were bloodshot. Seeing that, I understood he hadn't gone back to the hotel last night.
"Good morning.@k@vS08/13/321000603. ...Were you up all night?"
"Ah, good morning.@k@vS08/11/321100431. Had something on my mind, you see.@k@vS08/11/321100432. ...Oh?"
The moment he looked up at me, Ooishi-san tilted his head quizzically.
"What's the matter, Akasaka-san?@k@vS08/11/321100434. You seem to be in quite a foul mood so early in the morning."
"...It's hard to be in a good mood.@k@vS08/13/321000605. Take a look at this."
Saying that, I pulled out a chair two seats away, sat down, and dropped the newspapers and magazines I'd bought at the kiosk onto the table in front of Ooishi-san with a heavy thud.
Weeklies, morning papers, tabloids, gossip rags, women's magazines. ...Even a rough count put them at around twenty different publications.
"Oh?@k@vS08/11/321100436. My, my, you certainly bought quite a haul.@k@vS08/11/321100437. Let's see here..."
Ooishi-san picked up one of them, opened it, and traced the large headlines in the table of contents with evident interest.
Then he slowly began reading the articles inside.

"Hmm... 'Bizarre! 2,000 Dead in Remote Village!'@k@vS08/11/321100439. 'The Deepening Mystery of the Gas Disaster! ...Could It Be Man-Made?!'@k@vS08/11/321100440. 'Hinamizawa Village, Shrouded in Mystery... The Truth Revealed!' ...is it?@k@vS08/11/321100441. I see, I see."

"Yet for all their talk of 'revealing the truth,' every single one ends with 'the truth remains shrouded in darkness.'@k@vS08/13/321000607. On top of that, their arguments are baseless and illogical -- reading them seriously only deepens the confusion.@k@vS08/13/321000608. ...Some 'revelation' that is."

The proposed causes for the disaster ranged from the natural volcanic gas theory to outlandish national conspiracy theories, a mass suicide by a folk religion, and even -- of all things -- an alien invasion.
In short, every last one was a careless feature that treated the Great Hinamizawa Disaster as nothing more than entertainment.
"But they all have more or less the same content, don't they?@k@vS08/11/321100443. I wonder if the same writer penned all of them?@k@vS08/11/321100444. Nfu fu fu fu!"

"Who knows.@k@vS08/13/321000610. Either way, it's plainly obvious none of this came from firsthand investigation.@k@vS08/13/321000611. It's hard to believe this is the work of professional journalists being paid for their efforts."

"Now, now, don't take it so seriously.@k@vS08/11/321100446. Reporters these days are all like this.@k@vS08/11/321100447. The era of gathering information with your own two feet is long gone.@k@vS08/11/321100448. Na ha ha ha..."
"...But still, Ooishi-san."
"Oh, right, right.@k@vS08/11/321100450. There's this magazine too.@k@vS08/11/321100451. Care to have a look?"
Saying that, Ooishi-san showed me the cover of the magazine he'd been reading.
It was a particularly gaudy photo weekly. ...When I saw the name, I recognized it as the one that had been sold out at the kiosk I'd visited.

"Apparently it's the most popular weekly in the country right now.@k@vS08/11/321100453. Fast, cheap, and entertaining information.@k@vS08/11/321100454. ...Well, they've apparently been hit with plenty of lawsuits for defamation from their subjects in return, though."
"Hmm... the summer occult feature?@k@vS08/13/321000614. 'The Curse of Oyashiro-sama' -- what?"
I had been skimming idly, but the moment I saw that sentence and the article that followed, I caught my breath.
What was written there was no baseless, half-baked occult story like some urban legend.
People from Hinamizawa decorating their homes with 'eerie protective charms.'
And performing 'sinister rituals' in which they offered sacrifices while praying.
Furthermore, the 'series of tragedies that swept up those around them' in the aftermath --
All of it... had actually happened.
"W-wait a moment.@k@vS08/13/321000616. This can't be...?!"
"Oh yes, the details of our investigation have been published practically verbatim.@k@vS08/11/321100456. There's supposed to be a press blackout, so how on earth did they get hold of this?"
"This is a serious information leak!@k@vS08/13/321000618. Who could possibly have done this?!"

"Who knows?@k@vS08/11/321100458. But regardless of accuracy, considering that this string of incidents was never released to the public, it's possible someone on the police side is involved behind the scenes."
"Still, it hasn't been long since these mysterious deaths occurred, yet they have remarkably detailed information about the tragedies.@k@vS08/11/321100460. Nfu fu fu..."
"This is no time to be impressed!@k@vS08/13/321000620. Besides, wherever the article falls short on facts, it's been padded with the writer's sloppy speculation and outright fabrication, hasn't it?!"
To make matters worse, the prose was skillfully crafted and well-organized, creating the illusion that even the falsehoods carried a measure of credibility.
If someone who didn't know the truth were to read this... there was a very high chance they'd believe it wholeheartedly.
"Well, the general public does love that sort of story.@k@vS08/11/321100462. ...And framing it as a summer occult feature was a truly cunning move."
"Not only are they targeting the demographic that craves sensationalism over truth,@k@vS08/11/321100464. ...they're also deftly evading any responsibility for informational accuracy."

Moreover, the demographics presumed to favor this kind of information -- women and young people -- absorbed and spread it quickly, and their influence in shaping public perception was by no means negligible.

So if those groups were to receive and internalize what happened in Hinamizawa -- along with the delusions and superstitions surrounding it -- interpreting it all on their own terms and spreading it further, then --?
"At this rate, the negative image of people from Hinamizawa will keep spreading."
"...And if that happens, they'll feel increasingly alienated from society and refuse to cooperate with our investigation, won't they?"
"That's entirely possible.@k@vS08/11/321100466. ...But still, to think they could depict the 'traditional Oyashiro-sama purification rituals' in this much detail..."
"This reporter did thorough work for someone who isn't a local.@k@vS08/11/321100468. Arakawa-san, is it? ...Let's remember that name."
"...Their aims and values seem entirely different from ours, though."
I said that with a sullen expression as I took the magazine and spread it open.
Inside, between the articles, there were historical documents including a timeline of Hinamizawa, accompanied by black-and-white photographs.
'...Long ago, Hinamizawa was called "Onigafuchi Village"... and its inhabitants are said to carry the blood of demons.'
That was how the occult-tinged article began.
'In this cursed village, inexplicable and bizarre incidents had been occurring periodically on the same day each year for the past four years.'
'Four years ago, the dam construction site foreman was brutally murdered. Three years ago, the leading couple who championed the construction project fell to their deaths from a cliff.'
'And two years ago, the village shrine priest, who had maintained a neutral position on the construction, died of an unexplained illness --'
...Moreover, the incidents described matched the facts as we knew them.
The skill with which they had dug up these matters -- treated as classified information -- was, admittedly, impressive.
'...This year, a photographer visiting the village and a nurse at the local clinic were murdered.'

'And the day before the infamous disaster, the shrine maiden -- a young girl revered as the reincarnation of the village's guardian deity "Oyashiro-sama" -- was found brutally slain on the shrine grounds.'
(...They're talking about Rika-chan.)
That adorable smile I'd once seen floated unbidden into my mind.
'...The survivors from Hinamizawa believe this gas disaster was the curse of "Oyashiro-sama," grieving the death of this girl.'
'...And so they perform protective rituals to ward off the curse from themselves, offering "sacrifices" to appease the deity's wrath.'
'Sacrifices' that drove people to their deaths.
...And when that alone wasn't enough to appease the curse, others were dragged in as well.
Blood called for blood, and still more blood was shed. Tragedy masquerading under the name of 'protective ritual'...
The words were deftly manipulated, the content crafted to captivate the reader.
And then --
"...What?"
Toward the end of the article, it even mentioned that citizens' groups had begun mobilizing to demand the public disclosure of information about people from Hinamizawa.
And that the movement had already spread to municipal governments in surrounding prefectures...?! This had to be a joke!
Even allowing for some degree of exaggeration in the magazine's reporting, if the speed and force of this movement were real, it amounted to nothing less than persecution of people from Hinamizawa!!
"...Ooishi-san, what is this?!"

"I didn't expect the impact to have spread this far...@k@vS08/11/321100470. This is progressing faster than I anticipated.@k@vS08/11/321100471. If the major media outlets start reporting this information as well..."
"-- This is media hysteria!@k@vS08/13/321000629. This is turning into a witch hunt!"
SLAM! I brought my fist down on the table and groaned.
Ooishi-san appeared to be taking it calmly on the surface... but given the mountain of cigarettes from earlier, he was clearly anything but calm on the inside.
"Indeed... the way it's written, it makes all the people of Hinamizawa look like terrifying criminals."

"There's a saying -- 'If you see a stranger, assume they're a thief.'@k@vS08/11/321100474. ...Perhaps the new version will be, 'If you see someone from Hinamizawa, assume they're a killer'?@k@vS08/11/321100475. ...Na ha ha ha ha!"
"This is no time to be laughing about...!"
"-- Well, setting that aside.@k@vS08/11/321100477. ...Akasaka-san, what I'm getting at is that the timing seems suspiciously convenient."
"What... do you mean?"

"Well, doesn't it seem like the information is flowing just a bit too quickly?@k@vS08/11/321100479. And it's oddly methodical -- released in stages, and very specific.@k@vS08/11/321100480. Take this article, for instance."

Ooishi-san fixed me with a sharp gaze, then pulled out another magazine, opened it, and pointed to a specific passage. There, complete with a detailed diagram, the names of Hinamizawa's Three Great Families had been published.
'The Three Great Families, considered the most venerable houses in Hinamizawa, are said to be the bloodlines that most strongly inherited the blood of the demons --'
After that explanation... the names Furude, Sonozaki, and Kimiyoshi were listed.
The names had been partially redacted, to be sure, but supplementary explanations linked 'Furude' to 'divination,' 'Sonozaki' to 'entrails,' and 'Kimiyoshi' to 'demons,'
...making it thorough enough that anyone with a sharp mind would figure it out immediately.
"Why would they go so far as to explain even this...?"
"Wouldn't you say they've entered the 'endgame'?"

"Stirring up incidents large and small in various places, spreading rumors about the cause.@k@vS08/11/321100483. ...And then, for the grand finale, they stage a spectacular event and present it as 'the curse.'@k@vS08/11/321100484. ...Something along those lines?"

Come to think of it, ever since the incidents began, there had been what felt like deliberate guidance -- no, outright information manipulation -- through magazine outlets, designed to make people believe that was the cause.
Did that mean... whoever was leaking this information was doing so with deliberate intent?
"...Fear commands the attention of the public."
He was absolutely right. Fear appeals to human instinct like nothing else, commanding eyes and ears.
...And the speed at which it was happening was far from normal.
"Tragedy, you see... has a way of captivating people."
For a moment, those words that Ooishi-san had let slip so quietly left me speechless.
"What do you... mean by that?"
"Nfu fu fu."

"If this information has been manipulated, then our opponent must be frighteningly intelligent.@k@vS08/11/321100489. They must understand the human heart very well indeed.@k@vS08/11/321100490. ...Truly."
What captures people's interest, what they find entertaining, and what they fear...
Someone intimately acquainted with those movements of the heart... is that what he was saying?
"Isn't there a saying from the art of war?@k@vS08/11/321100492. To divert someone's attention, you attract it with something even more powerful... or so it goes."
"And meanwhile, the truth sinks into darkness --"
...In other words.
Ooishi-san was suggesting that the 'curse' was the work of human hands.
Implicit in his words was the inference that the Great Hinamizawa Disaster was the result of someone's conspiracy.

To hide the truth from the public, someone was deliberately making people perceive the disaster as an occult phenomenon, thereby eliminating any notion of human involvement -- he seemed convinced of that.
"And that brings us to... the 'endgame' I mentioned earlier."
"'Endgame'...?"
"They're... most likely planning to deliver the finishing blow."
'They'...? A finishing blow...?
What? ...What on earth could that be?
"Are you saying... there's still more to come?"
"Indeed.@k@vS08/11/321100497. The Three Great Families, popularly believed to carry the strongest demon blood, were not entirely wiped out by the gas disaster."
"...So even if a member of the Three Families escaped Hinamizawa, if 'Oyashiro-sama' were to relentlessly pursue them... and ultimately curse them to death..."
"People across the country would come to firmly believe that 'the curse is real'... wouldn't you agree?"

The Furude family had consisted solely of Rika-chan, so her death effectively ended the line. The key members of the Sonozaki family had all perished in the disaster as well, and their remaining relatives had completely vanished.
That meant the only family among the Three Great Families who had moved outside the village before the gas disaster, and whose current whereabouts were known, was --
"That's right.@k@vS08/11/321100501. There is a family from the Kimiyoshi line who relocated far from the village before the gas disaster..."
I pictured Natsumi-san's face -- the way she'd spoken so enthusiastically about books the other day.
Her smile overlapped with Rika-chan's...
"That girl from before... Natsumi Kimiyoshi-san's family?"
"Yes. The Kimiyoshi family may well be the closest to the next terrible incident that's about to unfold.@k@vS08/11/321100503. ...We'll need to step up our surveillance of them significantly."
"Surveillance...?"
"Yes.@k@vS08/11/321100505. If there truly is human involvement behind the curse,@k@vS08/11/321100506. ...don't you think that person would eventually make contact with the Kimiyoshi family?"
Indeed... Ooishi-san was right.

If someone wanted to demonstrate the terror of the disaster's curse as effectively and dramatically as possible, the probability of the Kimiyoshi family -- as one of the Three Great Families -- being targeted was extremely high.
And among them, who would they go after...?!
It went without saying. The girl born and raised in a family with deep ties to Hinamizawa, yet simultaneously living a life seemingly 'unconnected' to the village -- Natsumi Kimiyoshi!
...After all, since ancient times, young women had been considered the most fitting choice for a 'sacrifice' in ritual.
Even in the modern era, as respect for human rights had grown, that notion remained stubbornly rooted in people's hearts -- persisting almost as a kind of 'expectation.'
In that case... she would be the perfect material for staging the horrors of the curse while generating maximum public interest!!
"I... I-I d-don't... know...!"
...She had said, 'I don't know.' The curse had nothing to do with her, and that was most likely the truth.
However... if there truly existed people scheming to 'stage' the curse, they would seek to involve her regardless of her own wishes.
"...You don't mean, Ooishi-san!"
"That's exactly what I mean.@k@vS08/11/321100508. Now then, what should we do?@k@vS08/11/321100509. ...Tomoe-chan is visiting the Kimiyoshi household today, isn't she?"
...I recalled what had happened on Saturday.
In our impatience to obtain a crucial lead, we had blundered badly during our contact with Natsumi Kimiyoshi the other day.

Fortunately, we'd been lucky enough that she didn't discover we were detectives... but making further contact ourselves was clearly out of the question. So we'd arranged for Inspector Minai -- being a fellow woman -- to take over today's investigation of the Kimiyoshi household.
Of course... in exchange, Inspector Minai had given us a rather thorough dressing-down over the circumstances of the incident...
"...But, Ooishi-san.@k@vS08/28/320500460. And Inspector Akasaka."
Inspector Minai faced us with an expression even more stern than usual and spoke.

"I understand how you felt given the circumstances, but... you were both far too hasty this time.@k@vS08/28/320500462. One wrong move and you could have been charged with the abduction of a minor. You understand that?"
"...You're right, I apologize.@k@vS08/13/321000647. The intention was to draw out information through casual conversation, but..."
"Casual?@k@vS08/28/320500464. ...Two grown men questioning her -- that alone constitutes enormous psychological pressure on a girl."
"Her family would feel the same way, naturally.@k@vS08/28/320500466. Forgive me for being blunt, but... Inspector, how would you feel if your own daughter were in that position?"
When she invoked my daughter Miyuki as an example... I was left completely speechless.
If I imagined the same situation, I would certainly burn with fury toward those men.

"...A girl of that age is incredibly delicate.@k@vS08/28/320500468. Even if we police officers make contact strictly by the book, a single misstep could leave a scar that lasts a lifetime.@k@vS08/28/320500469. ...Please. Keep that in mind."
...Having been so thoroughly reprimanded by Inspector Minai, I genuinely reflected on how careless we had been.
I should have been far more cautious about taking a girl of such a sensitive age out for questioning...
"...Hoo boy, she really let us have it, didn't she?@k@vS08/11/321100511. Na ha ha ha."
"But... Inspector Minai's admonishment was entirely justified.@k@vS08/13/321000650. I regret not being more considerate in that regard."
"Well... in that girl's case, it's probably not just that."
Hearing Ooishi-san's suggestive murmur, I turned to look at him quizzically. ...But he said nothing more on the subject, and after glancing at the wall clock, he spoke.
"In any case, let's discuss this with Tomoe-chan when she gets back.@k@vS08/11/321100514. We can decide after that."
"Yes.@k@vS08/13/321000653. ...But will it be all right?@k@vS08/13/321000654. Inspector Minai hasn't seemed very enthusiastic about investigating the Hinamizawa survivors lately..."

"Well, it's been one unpleasant incident after another.@k@vS08/11/321100516. ...The one at the care center was especially bad.@k@vS08/11/321100517. The suspect and three residents all electrocuted to death together."
...After that bizarre incident, Inspector Minai returned from the scene and suggested to us that we discontinue the investigation.
It wasn't out of fear. ...It was likely because she had been forced to recognize that our strength alone was woefully insufficient to challenge the depth of the darkness behind these incidents.
We managed to persuade her somehow, and she agreed to continue cooperating, but...
"On top of that, after the kind of contact we made, I expect Natsumi Kimiyoshi will be completely on guard.@k@vS08/13/321000656. ...We've saddled Inspector Minai with a difficult task."
"Well, I'm sure Tomoe-chan will manage.@k@vS08/11/321100519. After all, she originally served in the juvenile division. I'd say she's far more experienced in these matters than we are."
"...Is that so?@k@vS08/13/321000658. In that case, all right."
"Oh no, the embarrassment is mine.@k@vS08/11/321100521. I got so excited at the prospect of obtaining valuable information that I rushed things.@k@vS08/11/321100522. Nfu fu fu..."
Saying that, Ooishi-san scratched his head looking somewhat chagrined.
He too had felt a sense of crisis and urgency from this string of incidents, and that frustration had driven him to press too hard, resulting in a blunder.

Even I hadn't been able to remain calm during the questioning of Natsumi-san. For Ooishi-san, who had been more closely involved with the disaster and the curse, the burden must have been even heavier...

"Akasaka-san.@k@vS08/11/321100524. Appearances aside, Tomoe-chan is someone who takes responsibility for her words.@k@vS08/11/321100525. Since she said she'd help us, I believe she'll follow through."
"So let's you and I take care of what we can on our end.@k@vS08/11/321100527. ...Agreed?"
"Yes, understood.@k@vS08/13/321000660. ...Oh, that's right."
I told Ooishi-san about the 'woman in a suit' the shopkeeper had mentioned earlier. Then we spread out a map and began searching for the locations of publishing companies in the area...
"...They sure dumped a troublesome job on me, huh."
Tomoe took a sip of the canned coffee she'd bought from a vending machine along the way, then looked up at the sky and muttered.
The rain from last night seemed like a lie -- the sun shone brilliantly in a clear blue sky. The post-rain humidity mingled with the late-morning freshness, occasionally carrying a cool breeze.
From the rows of green trees in the nearby grove, the raucous chorus of cicadas. Their sound rained down so ceaselessly that the word 'downpour' would have been an apt description.
"Now then, what should I do..."
She had already confirmed the location of the Kimiyoshi house. She'd also determined that the housewife, Haruko, was home.
She had likewise investigated her husband Touji's workplace and the community center that her mother Aki frequently visited.
In other words, she had come to this town thoroughly prepared... but.
"No matter how I look at it... the prospects seem slim."
She'd learned about Haruko's past from a 'separate document.' Based on that, it was easy to predict that the woman was not fond of the police -- in fact, she might even harbor outright hostility.
So even if she made contact, it was hard to imagine Haruko cooperating or providing information willingly.
Touji was the quintessential diligent salaryman -- leaving for work early in the morning and staying late with overtime.
He appeared to have no hobbies to speak of, and little in the way of private socializing with his colleagues.
Moreover, perhaps out of fear of his wife, he hardly ever stopped by bars, let alone the entertainment district.
So the approach of engineering a chance encounter and loosening his tongue with alcohol... was completely unusable. Another dead end.
And then there was Haruko's mother, Aki...
"...Out of the question."

She hadn't been particularly outgoing to begin with, and since moving to this unfamiliar town, she had become increasingly reclusive. She had few friends her own age, and apparently rarely spoke with anyone at the community center.
By rights, she was probably the person most likely to possess valuable information about the 'curse'... but if a complete stranger tried to make contact, she almost certainly wouldn't say a word.
"Which means... it comes back to 'that girl' after all."
With a heavy sigh, Tomoe tossed the empty can toward a nearby trash bin.
The can traced a clean arc, struck the rim with a light clang, and was swallowed inside.
(What a rotten role to be stuck with... honestly.)
Natsumi Kimiyoshi.
...She had probably lost many friends in the disaster.

For an older person, it might be easier to cope. But for a girl her age -- old enough to understand the situation -- the 'death' of people close to her must have placed an enormous burden on her mind and body.
That wasn't just idle speculation on her part.
She herself had gone through the same thing, and her sister had suffered wounds even deeper...
(She's a girl who should be comforted and cared for, more than anything...)
And yet Tomoe had to extract from her information about Hinamizawa -- the very source of her anguish. ...The contradiction was painful.
"...To be perfectly honest, I've been wanting to step away from this entire series of incidents."
After returning from the Sawatari care center where the incident had occurred, Tomoe pulled Akasaka and Ooishi into a separate room and confessed this to them, looking apologetic.
"...Inspector Minai, we are well aware of the impossible burden we're placing on you.@k@vS08/13/321000662. But --"

"This isn't about regretting my cooperation, or about responsibility.@k@vS08/28/320500479. I owe you a great deal, Ooishi-san, and I want to help as much as I can...@k@vS08/28/320500480. No, I wanted to."
"...Yes, and I am truly grateful for that.@k@vS08/11/321100529. Tomoe-chan."

"But,@k@vS08/28/320500482. ...over these past several days at least, all our investigating has produced almost no useful information,@k@vS08/28/320500483. ...and it even feels as though we may be making things worse."
"I, too, want to uncover the truth. And the bizarre behavior of those who blindly believe in the curse is certainly troubling."
"...Even so, if we ourselves are creating the triggers for these tragedies,@k@vS08/28/320500486. then perhaps our continued involvement isn't merely futile -- it may actually be harmful."
"...Inspector Minai."
"Yes... I understand how you feel, Tomoe-chan.@k@vS08/11/321100531. And knowing you, I know it's not timidity that's driving this."
Ooishi nodded gently, without his usual levity.
...Akasaka could understand Inspector Minai's feelings all too painfully well. Tragedies had struck at the very houses they had investigated, as if their actions had drawn them there.
Seeing that firsthand... anyone would begin to suspect they might be the cause, and hesitate to take another step.

"But, Tomoe-chan.@k@vS08/11/321100533. ...In just these past few days alone, there have been multiple assault incidents.@k@vS08/11/321100534. And the resulting friction with neighboring residents.@k@vS08/11/321100535. Even if we withdraw now, the turmoil won't stop."
"...You're saying there's no turning back?"
"More precisely... it feels to me as though they're threatening us to abandon our investigation.@k@vS08/11/321100537. And if that's the case, backing down here is not an option."
"...True enough.@k@vS08/28/320500489. Ours is a profession where showing weakness means losing."
"I sincerely regret dragging you into this.@k@vS08/11/321100539. ...But even so, I can't bring myself to withdraw.@k@vS08/11/321100540. My pride as a detective won't allow it."
"Ooishi-san..."
"Please.@k@vS08/11/321100542. ...Could you lend me your strength just a little longer?"
Tomoe shook her head lightly, then raised the face she had been keeping downcast.
She had never seen Ooishi so earnestly persistent before. And she was not so cold-hearted as to brush that aside.
(In any case, standing here agonizing about it won't help.)
She would start by visiting the school Natsumi Kimiyoshi attended. And from there...
Just then.
Her gaze happened to wander toward a corner of the park, where she spotted a solitary figure sitting on one of the benches.
The reddish tint of the clothing stood out even at a distance -- clearly a school uniform. And...
"...Natsumi... Kimiyoshi-san."

Murmuring the name, Tomoe pulled out the photograph she kept in her inside pocket and confirmed. ...There was no mistaking it. The girl sitting there was none other than Natsumi, the only daughter of the Kimiyoshi family.
She glanced at her watch. ...It was already past nine, the minute hand beyond the halfway mark.
Affecting the air of a passerby, she walked over to Natsumi. The girl sat with a dark expression, head bowed as if lost in thought... showing no sign of noticing Tomoe's approach.
(Did she leave school early? ...Or has she stopped going altogether?)
She hadn't done a proper preliminary investigation, but she'd heard nothing about Natsumi being bullied at school.
Her behavior was said to be exemplary, with no habit of truancy. ...Which meant the recent media coverage about Hinamizawa was likely the cause.
"...All right. Let's do this."
She had originally planned to wait and intercept Natsumi on her way home from school, but having her here alone without any classmates around was actually more convenient.

There was no telling whether she could extract useful information, but feeling she should at least fulfill her basic duty, Tomoe bought two cans of juice from a nearby vending machine and headed for the bench where Natsumi sat.
And then,
"-- Hello."
"Huh? U-um..."
Startled by the sudden greeting, Natsumi jerked her head up as if she'd been jolted. Seeing her bewildered expression, Tomoe carefully put on a cheerful smile so as not to frighten her, and spoke.
"This seat... taken?"
"Oh, n-no..."
"Thanks."
After a light bow, Tomoe sat down and let out a breath. She opened one of the juice cans in her hand and took a small sip.
...The lingering taste of the coffee she'd just had made it a bit odd, but she endured it.
"Sorry... am I in the way?"
"Huh? N-no, not at all..."
Though clearly ill at ease, Natsumi shook her head and answered.
She seemed uncertain whether she should get up and leave of her own accord. ...Just as she'd been told -- a timid personality.
In that case...
"Sorry for the sudden intrusion.@k@vS08/28/320500502. It just felt nostalgic, you know... that uniform."
"Actually, I'm an alumna of your school.@k@vS08/28/320500504. So that makes me your senpai, I guess.@k@vS08/28/320500505. ...Is Nakamura in the math department still around?"
"Nakamura-sensei... you said?@k@vS08/36/320100165. No, there's no teacher by that name..."
"...I see, so she transferred already.@k@vS08/28/320500507. She was such a nag, but knowing she's gone... it's a little sad."
Tomoe lowered her eyes slightly and gave a wistful smile. ...Of course, it was all a fabrication.

From her years in the juvenile division, she knew better than to blurt out 'Shouldn't you be in school?' That would only frighten the girl or provoke defiance, making it impossible for the message to get through.

So in situations like this, you brought yourself down to the young person's eye level to close the distance, and then persuaded them. ...That was the 'method' of guidance she'd learned in the juvenile division.
Students who skipped school while still wearing their uniforms, in particular, often carried a sense of guilt about their truancy.
As a result, they had a strong tendency to seek out kindred spirits or sympathetic ears, and even a complete stranger bringing up common ground could put them at ease.
"Even so... it's still lying."
Early in her assignment, she had said exactly that and pushed back against her superiors' orders. So personally, Tomoe still felt both hesitation and guilt about this approach.
However... ironically, there was no denying its effectiveness.
So she told herself it was all for the sake of opening the hearts of young people on the verge of going astray, and over the years she had managed to steer more than a few away from delinquency...
"Ah, sorry for rambling on like that.@k@vS08/28/320500510. ...Oh, I hit the jackpot on the vending machine roulette earlier -- here, if you'd like one."
"Ah, no, that's fine.@k@vS08/36/320100169. Um... you're not going to scold me?"
"For what?"
"Well, I mean... I'm skipping school..."

"Ahahahaha.@k@vS08/28/320500513. Actually, I'm playing hooky from work right now.@k@vS08/28/320500514. Making sales rounds in this heat?@k@vS08/28/320500515. Seriously, try walking in my shoes for once, you lousy boss."
"So you and I are in the same boat.@k@vS08/28/320500517. What do you say -- shall we share a drink?"

The rigidity born of wariness and confusion softened just slightly in Natsumi's expression. Seeing that, Tomoe felt her playful side take over, and she suddenly pressed the cold juice can against Natsumi's neck.
"Eek, that's cold?!@k@vS08/36/320100174. Wh-what are you doing~?"
"Ahahahaha!@k@vS08/28/320500519. See? Nice and cold, right?@k@vS08/28/320500520. I can't drink two by myself, so better have it before it gets warm, yeah?"
Natsumi let out a wry, deflated laugh and accepted the juice from Tomoe -- 'reluctantly,' on the surface. She popped the tab and took a gulp.
"...It's good."
"Great.@k@vS08/28/320500522. Do you like orange juice?"
"Ah, yes.@k@vS08/36/320100178. I'm not really good with carbonation though..."
"Yeah, I know what you mean.@k@vS08/28/320500524. That fizzy feeling in your throat, right?@k@vS08/28/320500525. Everyone around me says it's delicious, but I don't see the appeal."
"I know, right?@k@vS08/36/320100180. Juice should be sweet and just... normally cold. That's much easier to drink."
"Oh? We really do think alike.@k@vS08/28/320500527. Ah, I'm Tomoe, by the way.@k@vS08/28/320500528. Tomoe Minai. Nice to meet you."
"Oh, I'm Natsumi.@k@vS08/36/320100182. Nice to meet you too."
Natsumi had let her guard down completely, and a smile gradually blossomed on her face. ...She was cute when she smiled, Tomoe thought, as her own expression relaxed in turn.
(Now then... how do I steer the conversation?)
While making harmless small talk about school and offering appropriate responses to keep the mood light, Tomoe racked her brain over when and how to bring up the topic of 'Hinamizawa.'

If this were simply a matter of addressing the truancy, she could ease into a conversation about home life at the right moment. ...But this time, the questioning of Natsumi was part of an investigation.
How to draw out information without arousing suspicion... the difficulty was extreme. One wrong word could undo all the atmosphere-building in an instant.
(But how on earth do I naturally bring up 'Hinamizawa'...?)
Just then.
Her gaze happened to drift to the side of the bench where Natsumi sat, and she noticed a plastic bag packed full of newspapers.
And poking out from the bag, one front page bore the headline 'Investigating the Great Hinamizawa Disaster' in bold print.
"Hey... what's with that newspaper?"
It had been an offhand, almost unconscious question, but Natsumi's relaxed expression froze instantly, and she shrank back in on herself. She hurriedly closed the bag as if to hide its contents.
"U-um... this is..."
"You bought quite a few.@k@vS08/28/320500535. Does your father read those?"
"N-no... my mom was the one who bought--"
She forced out just that much, almost groaning, and then fell silent.
...Sensing the discomfort in her manner, Tomoe peered up at the girl's downcast face from below and spoke in as gentle a tone as she could muster, hoping to find a thread to pull.
"Hey, Natsumi-chan.@k@vS08/28/320500537. Could I have a quick look at that newspaper?"
"Huh?"
"Yeah, there's a special program I wanted to catch tonight.@k@vS08/28/320500539. Let me just check the --"
"N-no, you can't!!"
Her outstretched hand was swatted away with force, and Tomoe stared, dumbfounded.
Seeing Tomoe's reaction, Natsumi came back to herself with a start and averted her face in embarrassment... but the instant she looked away, she drew a sharp breath with a shocked expression.
"Huh? Oh..."
Only then did Tomoe feel a cold sensation on her feet and turn her gaze downward.
She saw that the juice can she'd been holding had fallen when her hand was knocked away, rolled across the ground, and its half-finished contents had splashed her shoes.
"I-I'll wipe it up right away!@k@vS08/36/320100192. I'm really sorry!"
"Ah... don't worry about it.@k@vS08/28/320500543. This much is nothing--"
But Natsumi pulled a tissue from her pocket, crouched down, and carefully wiped Tomoe's shoe tips without a thought for dirtying her own hands.
...Tomoe watched her, all the fight drained from her expression.
(She really is a good kid, isn't she...)
The more she felt it... the more her heart ached.
Natsumi's guilt-laden, self-isolating reactions were almost certainly connected to 'Hinamizawa.'
And yet, she had done nothing wrong. Why should she have to suffer so much simply for being from 'Hinamizawa'...?
"I'm sorry, I... I should..."
"The Great Hinamizawa Disaster, huh.@k@vS08/28/320500546. ...Newspapers can be really selfish."
At Tomoe's murmured words, Natsumi froze mid-motion as she'd been about to stand.
...They were her true feelings, spoken before she could stop them.
She felt a twinge of regret after saying it, but... gazing up at the sky, Tomoe continued, haltingly.
"I don't know what this 'curse' business is about,@k@vS08/28/320500548. ...but what do they think a disaster that killed 2,000 people is -- entertainment?"

"There are still so many people out there grieving, suffering through the loss of friends and family,@k@vS08/28/320500550. and yet these people keep churning out careless articles... I can't stand it."
"Newspapers, magazines, TV news programs.@k@vS08/28/320500552. ...For the people who make those things, other people's misfortune is just a 'product.'"
"They force people to dredge up things they'd rather forget, and they don't have the slightest understanding of what those people are feeling as they grieve and suffer..."

"If it were factual reporting, I could still accept it.@k@vS08/28/320500555. But they take what really happened and twist it for entertainment, force-fit explanations onto it, fabricate things outright..."

"They never stop to think about how much pain the victims, their families, and friends endure seeing that.@k@vS08/28/320500557. They hurt people and then have the gall to talk about the 'justice of the press.'@k@vS08/28/320500558. ...It's enough to make you sick."
"...Tomoe-san."
For Tomoe, this was anything but someone else's problem. ...Her mind went back twelve years, to the incident when her parents died in a suspicious fire at their home.

At the time, it wasn't only their parents' death that tormented Tomoe and Madoka. ...For a long while afterward, they were also tormented by the sensationalized media coverage that treated the incident as entertainment.

Her parents' death, her sister's hospitalization, and her own future... In the midst of desperately struggling to survive one calamity after another, Tomoe was besieged by countless reporters seeking interviews.
Asking things like, 'Why was your detective father targeted?' 'What conspiracy lurks behind the investigation he was working on?'...

Tomoe, who knew nothing of the truth, could only give vague answers -- but to her astonishment, her words were rewritten without permission, twisted into grudges against the criminal and condemnations of the police... content utterly different from anything she had said.
(It was nothing profound. ...They just wanted to use us as a pretext to criticize the police -- the establishment.)

Then there was an article claiming that Tomoe herself was the actual target -- that someone jealous of her growing fame as a high school track athlete had set the fire as a threat, and her parents' deaths were merely unfortunate collateral...
...Because of that, the police ended up interrogating the rivals she'd competed alongside in friendly rivalry, who then resented her for disrupting their training.
And to cap it all off, someone had pointed out that Madoka happened to come home late the day of the incident and was going through a rebellious phase, casting groundless suspicion on her...
"...So they're saying Mom and Dad died because of us?!@k@vS08/28/320500561. Don't be ridiculous!!"
They would never know -- and never care to know -- how much those articles had burdened the two sisters with grief so crushing it nearly destroyed them.
And once the readers and viewers lost interest, the media would move on to their next topic without a backward glance at the girls they'd left in their wake...
Of course, she didn't believe all media was like that. But Tomoe had never been able to rid herself of the revulsion she felt toward certain outlets.
"They say 'The pen is mightier than the sword,' but it's not just that.@k@vS08/28/320500563. 'The pen is crueler than the sword' would be more accurate."

"...Though in a society ruled by majority opinion, it probably doesn't register as much of a problem.@k@vS08/28/320500565. As long as the masses are entertained, who cares about a handful of people in the minority... right?"
"...Oh. S-sorry.@k@vS08/28/320500567. I got carried away."
"No.@k@vS08/36/320100200. What you're saying, Tomoe-san... I think I understand, somehow."
Natsumi rolled up the tissue and set it beside the bench, then settled back into her seat and spoke.
"It's easy to make a fuss about things you don't even understand, making assumptions from within your own limited frame of reference, isn't it?"
"They couldn't care less about the circumstances or situation of the people they're talking about.@k@vS08/36/320100203. In the end, everyone puts themselves first."
"Natsumi-san..."
With a thin, self-deprecating smile, Natsumi looked up at the sky and spoke.

"...I think there's something wrong with the people on the receiving end too.@k@vS08/36/320100206. Blindly believing, making assumptions on their own...@k@vS08/36/320100207. And then getting betrayed and hurt."
"...Then you should just stop believing from the start.@k@vS08/36/320100209. Keep everyone at arm's length, and if you stay alone, there's nothing to worry about."

"...Besides, if you clear your mind and ask yourself why someone is reaching out to you, the answer usually becomes obvious.@k@vS08/36/320100211. In the end, it's just self-interest.@k@vS08/36/320100212. ...Isn't it?"
"That's... but."

"Even when they put on a show of sympathy, they're really just amusing themselves.@k@vS08/36/320100214. Getting drunk on their own performance of kindness.@k@vS08/36/320100215. I'd rather be ignored entirely."
"It feels like being looked down on. Maybe they're satisfied thinking they're being considerate, but... every time they flaunt that sense of superiority, it infuriates me."

"They don't know the pain of my wounds, yet they have no idea how much they're hurting me.@k@vS08/36/320100218. There's no way they could understand.@k@vS08/36/320100219. ...I'll be damned if they ever do!"

"Because we're friends?@k@vS08/36/320100221. Ha! Don't make me laugh!@k@vS08/36/320100222. They help you when it suits them... and the moment they realize it's beyond them, they turn their backs and pretend they never knew."
"I had my hopes up, you know?@k@vS08/36/320100224. I thought maybe, just maybe, they'd actually help."
"Can you imagine how it feels to have that hope betrayed?@k@vS08/36/320100226. ...Then it's better not to hope at all from the start!"
"Kimiyoshi-sa-- !"
Tomoe was horrified -- she'd let slip a name she wasn't supposed to know. ...But Natsumi showed no surprise, turning to her with a sardonic smile instead.
"...Ah, so you really are a detective."

"I had a feeling.@k@vS08/36/320100229. So tell me, how much of everything you said was true?@k@vS08/36/320100230. Maybe it was all lies?@k@vS08/36/320100231. Were you planning to trick me into giving up information?"
"Ahahahaha, you're quite the con artist."

"You're all the same.@k@vS08/36/320100234. You approach me with your fake warmth and kindness, and then betray me.@k@vS08/36/320100235. What could you possibly want to know about Hinamizawa so badly that you'd stoop to such filthy, despicable tactics?@k@vS08/36/320100236. Well? Hm?"
"Ahahahahahahaha."
"N-no, that's not it! I--"
"SHUT UP!!!"
At the sudden roar... Tomoe caught her breath and froze.
The gentle atmosphere vanished in an instant.
Countless sharp needles, rigid with terror, drove into her body, paralyzing her limbs.
She couldn't even... breathe.
She felt as though all the blood in her body was draining away with an audible rush, and her consciousness began to dim.
Fighting desperately to hold onto her sanity... Tomoe stared back at the girl before her.
The timid Natsumi Kimiyoshi from moments ago... was nowhere to be seen.
Eyes bulging with terrifying intensity... bared canines ready to sink into flesh at any moment... It was the very visage of a 'demon.'
Beads of sweat welled up like marbles on her forehead, streaming down and dripping from the tip of her chin.
The illusion that time had stopped.
She could no longer hear any of the sounds that should have been rustling around her.
Her field of vision warped and twisted, strange colors bleeding together as an unpleasant light flickered repeatedly, as if trying to erase every contour of the world...
She finally came back to herself, and desperately fought the urge to recoil, clenching her trembling fists with every ounce of strength she had.
"Your lies aside, I'm glad you listened to me.@k@vS08/36/320100243. That much was genuine.@k@vS08/36/320100244. ...Well then, goodbye."
"W-wait, Natsumi-san!!"
As Natsumi picked up the bag with unhurried movements, rose from the bench, and began walking away, Tomoe found herself crying out after her.
Natsumi, already several steps ahead, stopped in her tracks -- her back still turned.
"What is it?@k@vS08/36/320100247. Do you want to know about Hinamizawa too?"

"...Unfortunately, if you want to know, you'll have to look elsewhere.@k@vS08/36/320100249. Though I doubt you'll find much beyond what's already in the official records.@k@vS08/36/320100250. And asking me certainly won't get you anything you're hoping for--"
"...That's not it, that's not what I-- !"
With everything she had.
Forgetting her position as a detective entirely, Tomoe spoke.
Her tongue nearly tripped over itself... her throat constricted, and she gasped for breath again and again.
Even so -- as someone who carried the same pain.
She could not stand by in silence while a girl was about to be swallowed by the same 'darkness' she had known.

"I'm sorry for deceiving you... truly!@k@vS08/28/320500579. But, Natsumi-san.@k@vS08/28/320500580. Are you really all right like this?@k@vS08/28/320500581. Believing in nothing, doubting everything... is that truly what you want?"
"...Whether I want it or not doesn't matter.@k@vS08/36/320100252. This is the only correct path. The only one.@k@vS08/36/320100253. Right?"
"...Really?@k@vS08/28/320500583. That's not how it looks to me.@k@vS08/28/320500584. Because you don't actually believe that, do you?"
"You're just trying to convince yourself.@k@vS08/28/320500586. You've fabricated this world populated entirely by villains and convinced yourself it's all there is!"
"...That's quite the assumption.@k@vS08/36/320100255. What could you possibly know about me...?"
"I do know!@k@vS08/28/320500588. I told you -- I was once the same as you!!"

"I too fell into despair, just like you, and lost the ability to believe in anything.@k@vS08/28/320500590. I agonized alone, I suffered alone, I cursed the entire world for singling me out for such misery...!"

"But... but, listen!@k@vS08/28/320500592. Not being able to believe in anything... is truly, unbearably painful.@k@vS08/28/320500593. The more I doubted, the more the people around me stopped trusting me, and they drifted away, one after another.@k@vS08/28/320500594. ...All of them!"
"That's why I was always alone -- lonely, frightened, and sad...!@k@vS08/28/320500596. Natsumi-san, can you really endure that?@k@vS08/28/320500597. I couldn't!"
...Tomoe herself, just once.
Just once, she had given in to total despair... and wished for everything to end.
The desperate studying to keep up. The part-time jobs to earn a living. ...And taking care of her sister.
She kept telling herself... that she didn't find it painful.
But... whenever she saw other girls her age enjoying themselves at Christmas or New Year's, she couldn't deny the loneliness that crept in... or even the envy.
Still, she believed that her efforts would lead to happiness someday. So she kept pushing, and pushing, and pushing forward...
She endured days that left no room to play, hardly even to catch her breath -- and then one day, her beloved sister left a note and vanished from her life.
The grief... and the despair she'd felt then still visited her in dreams from time to time.
Beaten by cold rain.
Dragging one shoe -- having lost the other somewhere -- through asphalt alleyways until she stumbled and collapsed from sheer exhaustion. And then --
"...Damn it, dammit...!!"
She couldn't bear any of it anymore.
Rain and tears streaming down her mud-smeared face... she poured those dark, blood-curdling emotions into her sobs... and Tomoe cried until her voice was raw.
Why?
Why am I the only one who has to endure this?
What did I do?
I gave up everything I wanted, endured so much, and worked so hard -- and this is what it amounts to?
'She' lives however she pleases... without a thought for my feelings. So why me?!
Then... what am I struggling for? What am I enduring all this for?
If I can never find happiness, then whose sake is all this suffering and sorrow for?!
Yes. Let's just end it. All of it. Everything.
Let whatever happens, happen. This worthless body, this worthless heart -- take them, anyone, I don't care...!
She was on the very brink of thinking that... right at the edge.
"...Oh my, what's the matter, young lady?@k@vS08/11/321100544. You'll catch cold sleeping in a place like this.@k@vS08/11/321100545. Nfu fu fu..."
...She had never forgotten that kindness, even to this day.
Thanks to 'him,' Tomoe had been introduced to people connected to her parents' case, and that had led her to where she stood now...
"If that hadn't happened... I don't know what would have become of me."

"But that's when I understood.@k@vS08/28/320500604. There will always be people who betray you -- but there are also, without question, people who treasure you!@k@vS08/28/320500605. As long as you never give up!"
"Even now, there are times when I'm afraid that the people closest to me might betray me!@k@vS08/28/320500607. But the world isn't made only of coldness!"
"And yes, there might be calculation or even malice behind someone's kindness,@k@vS08/28/320500609. ...but the warmth of that kindness itself -- that much is real!"
"...Pretty words.@k@vS08/36/320100260. They make me sick."

"So what if they're pretty words!@k@vS08/28/320500611. If the alternative is looking back on your life in your final moments and wanting to paint every memory black, then give me the pretty words any day!"
"You, who have never even tried -- who have never even thought of trying -- don't get to say that to me! I won't hear it!@k@vS08/28/320500611_2. Don't you dare deny the hell I've been through!!"
At Tomoe's torrent of words... Natsumi's shoulders, still turned away, twitched -- just slightly, as if in response.
In that moment... Tomoe finally realized she was crying.
And as she gazed at that small, blurring silhouette... she wanted to believe that her words had reached the girl, even if only a little.

"Hey... Natsumi-san.@k@vS08/28/320500614. Don't think... that you can't believe in anyone.@k@vS08/28/320500615. There must be people who show you kindness, right?@k@vS08/28/320500616. Friends, family... those people, I'm sure they--"
"-- There aren't any."
Slowly.
Natsumi turned to face Tomoe.
...The same cold, hollow eyes as before... devoid of light.
But.
Somewhere deep within them, Tomoe thought she glimpsed something... a wavering.
"Family... is something I don't have.@k@vS08/36/320100265. People who would steal my happiness away... are not my family."
"Natsumi... san..."
"...Tomoe-san.@k@vS08/36/320100267. I wish I could have met you sooner.@k@vS08/36/320100268. If I had, then maybe..."
"...Goodbye.@k@vS08/36/320100270. I'm glad... we got to talk."
And this time, Natsumi walked away without looking back even once.
For a while... Tomoe simply stood there, staring desolately at the park entrance.
How much time had passed?
Wracked by a sense of futility and helplessness, Tomoe sat slumped on the bench.
She'd gotten emotional with Natsumi and ended up telling her own story. ...The girl must have thought her pathetic.
And in the end, she had failed to obtain a single piece of useful information...
Having resigned herself to returning to the station, Tomoe was getting up from the bench when she felt something off and shifted her weight to the side.
There sat a cute little pink plastic case.
"A compact? ...No, a pill case?"
Opening the lid, she found two packages of six capsules each stored inside.
She checked the pharmaceutical code on the back... but the numbers and letters printed there were ones she'd never seen before.
"Who does this belong to... ah."
With the packages removed, she could see writing on the bottom of the case. 'Natsumi Kimiyoshi' was written there in neat handwriting.
Most likely... it had fallen from Natsumi's pocket when she pulled out the tissue to wipe Tomoe's shoes.
"If she keeps it in a case like this... is it regular medication?"
If so, she needed to get it back to her quickly.
With that thought, Tomoe stood up and started running after Natsumi.
...But almost simultaneously, the pager in her breast pocket let out an grating tone.
"...Oh, what terrible timing.@k@vS08/28/320500626. Let's see..."
With no choice, Tomoe dashed into a nearby unoccupied phone booth, picked up the receiver, dropped in a ten-yen coin, and called the First Investigation Division at Kakiuchi Station.
"Hello, this is the First Criminal Investigation Division--"
"Fujita-kun?@k@vS08/28/320500628. It's Minai.@k@vS08/28/320500629. I got paged..."
"...What did you say?@k@vS08/28/320500631. Another incident at the Hatakeyama house?!@k@vS08/28/320500632. What are the details?"
Her eyes wide with shock... Tomoe set the receiver back on the hook.
"How... could this...!"
The report Fujita had delivered.
It was that the Hatakeyama family from the previous incident had carried out a murder-suicide with a hunting rifle...!
Later -- July 6th, Showa 58 (Wednesday)
changeReason: Date format changed from Western calendar year to Japanese Showa era dating, which alters how the reader contextualizes the time period.

TextENGNew: "...I see.@k@vS08/13/321000665. So that's what happened."
"Yes.@k@vS08/28/320500635. ...I'm truly sorry I couldn't accomplish more."
Gripping the steering wheel and keeping her eyes fixed on the road ahead, Inspector Minai recounted the situation to us in the back seat.
"Now, don't be so hard on yourself.@k@vS08/11/321100547. If you couldn't manage it, Tomoe-chan, we'd have gotten even less done.@k@vS08/11/321100548. Isn't that right, Akasaka-san?"
"Yes, absolutely."
I agreed with genuine conviction.
And I could only hope... that Inspector Minai's heartfelt words had reached Natsumi, at least in some small way.
"Even so..."
"What is it?"
"They use words like 'snapping' on television these days,@k@vS08/28/320500638. ...but kids really are complicated, aren't they."
"Snapping...?"
Inspector Minai tilted her head as she described how Natsumi Kimiyoshi had suddenly erupted in fury during their recent encounter.

"The people at her school and neighborhood described her as a shy, quiet girl, but... you never know.@k@vS08/28/320500640. I was genuinely surprised that she had the spirit to raise her voice like that."
"Spirit...?"
Something about that characterization struck me as off.

I had only met her once, but... I hadn't sensed even a trace of that kind of spirit. In fact, could a girl who lacked the willpower to walk away even when she was clearly uncomfortable really be called spirited?

"Well, the mother apparently has quite a reputation for her strong temperament.@k@vS08/11/321100550. It's only natural for a daughter to take after her parent in personality.@k@vS08/11/321100551. Right, Akasaka-san?"
"Huh? Yes, I suppose so..."
But I wondered if that was truly the explanation.
Just then.
"...I want to help her.@k@vS08/28/320500642. That girl--"
"Huh?"
At Inspector Minai's quiet murmur, I looked up.
Through the rearview mirror, I could see her eyes fixed on the road ahead... but there was a certain lonely glimmer in them.
"She reminds me of how I used to be, somehow...@k@vS08/28/320500644. If my circumstances had been a little different, I might have ended up the same way. So..."
"Tomoe-chan..."
Silence.
The car made its way toward Kakiuchi Station at a gentle pace.
In the quiet that followed, the three of us spoke no words... each lost in our own thoughts.
